description: The flower of a plant, especially one producing fruit; also the act of flowering. 'Phiralea' names the moment of emergence—when buds break into color, when potential becomes visible beauty, the flowering that precedes fruiting.
sound_symbolism: The word opens into bloom. 'Phi' /ɸi/ begins with soft breath, the bud stirring. Then 'ra' reaches outward, petals extending. 'Le' lightens as the flower spreads. Finally 'a' opens fully, the blossom complete and offered to the world.
grammatical_notes: Noun: 'lo phiralea' (blossoms); takes the living classifier 'lipha' when counted. The flowering itself is a verb of its own: 'peloru sheloa.' (The flower blooms.) Related to 'peloru' (flower), but phiralea names the moment of flowering as a thing beheld.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Blossoming teaches that beauty emerges in its own time—forcing hastens nothing, while patient conditions allow flowering to occur naturally.
pillars.art-nouveau-aesthetics: Blossoms were the supreme motif of Art Nouveau—the moment of opening, the curve of petals, the ephemeral beauty that must be honored before it passes.
